title: Listening Starter Mock 02 — Diabetes Screening + Asthma Review

extracts.title: Mental Health in Primary Care Talk

questions.explanation: GP records fasting glucose of 7.2 mmol/L, above the diagnostic threshold.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Your fasting glucose came back at 7.2 millimoles per litre.

questions.explanation: Patient reports his father has type 2 diabetes.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: My father has it — type 2 as well.

questions.explanation: BMI measured at 31, classifying the patient as obese.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: His BMI is 31 — that puts him in the obese range.

questions.explanation: HbA1c of 52 mmol/mol confirms the diagnosis of type 2 diabetes.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: The HbA1c is 52 — that confirms type 2 diabetes.

questions.explanation: Patient identifies excessive thirst as his most troublesome symptom.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I've been incredibly thirsty, all the time.

questions.stem: Dietary habit noted: high intake of ____
questions.explanation: Patient admits to drinking several sugary drinks daily.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I do drink quite a few fizzy drinks every day.

questions.explanation: GP refers patient to a dietician for dietary education.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I'm going to refer you to our dietician.

questions.explanation: GP prescribes metformin as first-line pharmacotherapy.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I'm starting you on metformin.

questions.explanation: Initial metformin dose is 500 mg twice daily.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: We'll start at 500 milligrams, twice a day.

questions.explanation: GP schedules three-monthly reviews.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I'd like to see you every three months initially.

questions.explanation: GP sets a 5 kg weight-loss target as a lifestyle goal.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Aim to lose around five kilograms over the next few months.

questions.explanation: BP measured at 138/90 during the consultation.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Blood pressure is 138 over 90 today.

questions.explanation: Patient states cold air is the main trigger for her asthma symptoms.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: It's mainly the cold air that sets it off.

questions.explanation: Patient uses salbutamol as her short-acting rescue inhaler.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I use the salbutamol one — the blue one.

questions.explanation: Patient is prescribed beclometasone as her preventer inhaler.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Your preventer is beclometasone — the brown inhaler.

questions.explanation: Peak flow measured at 380 L/min during the review.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Your peak flow today is 380 litres per minute.

questions.explanation: Patient reports waking twice per week with breathlessness.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I'm waking up twice a week with tightness.

questions.explanation: Patient missed three days of teaching due to asthma.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I missed about three days last term because of it.

questions.explanation: Nurse notes patient is not using a spacer device with her preventer.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: You're not using a spacer — that would really help.

questions.explanation: Nurse steps therapy up to a medium dose of inhaled corticosteroid.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: We'll step you up to the medium dose.

questions.explanation: Patient referred for spirometry to confirm diagnosis and severity.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I'd like to refer you for spirometry.

questions.explanation: The written asthma action plan is updated during the review.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: We've updated your action plan today.

questions.explanation: Next review booked for eight weeks' time.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Come back and see me in eight weeks.

questions.explanation: Patient asks whether her inhalers are safe if she becomes pregnant.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Is the inhaler safe if I get pregnant?

questions.stem: You hear a ward sister briefing a healthcare assistant. The main instruction is to
options: reposition the patient every four hours
questions.explanation: Ward sister specifically asks the HCA to document fluid intake every hour.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Make sure you're recording the fluid intake every hour without fail.
questions.distractorExplanation: Observations and repositioning are routine tasks mentioned in passing; the explicit instruction is hourly fluid recording.
optionDistractorWhy: Mentioned as ongoing practice, not the specific instruction given.
Standard pressure-area care — not the focus of the briefing.

questions.stem: A hospital pharmacist tells a nurse that the priority is to
options: use the new drug chart format
contact the prescriber about the dose
questions.correctAnswer: use the new drug chart format
questions.explanation: Pharmacist instructs the nurse to switch to the updated drug chart format effective immediately.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: From today we're using the new chart format — please make sure all the team knows.
questions.distractorExplanation: Allergy checks are always required but were not the pharmacist's message; prescriber contact is not requested.
optionDistractorWhy: A standard requirement not related to this specific communication.
No prescriber query is raised — the dose is acceptable.

questions.stem: During a charge nurse handover, the reason the patient cannot be discharged today is
questions.explanation: Charge nurse states discharge is on hold pending outstanding CT scan results.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: We're holding discharge until the CT results come back.
questions.distractorExplanation: Family availability and physiotherapy are not cited as barriers in this handover.
optionDistractorWhy: Family contact has already been made — not the reason for delay.
Physiotherapy was completed this morning per the notes.

questions.stem: A GP receptionist tells a colleague that the action needed is to
options: book a locum for the afternoon
questions.explanation: Receptionist asks colleague to contact patients and reschedule the three afternoon slots.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Can you ring those three afternoon patients and find them new slots?
questions.distractorExplanation: The morning clinic is unaffected; a locum is not mentioned as a solution.
optionDistractorWhy: Morning clinic is running as normal — not the issue.
No locum is sought; the appointments are simply rescheduled.

questions.stem: A senior nurse tells a student nurse that the error made was
questions.explanation: Senior nurse identifies that the student failed to confirm allergy status before administration.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: You never checked the allergy status — that's the step you missed.
questions.distractorExplanation: Dose and site were both correct; the procedural gap was the allergy check.
optionDistractorWhy: The dose was correct — this is a distractor drawn from common medication errors.
The correct site was used — not mentioned as an issue.

questions.stem: A team leader tells a community nurse that the priority visit today is
questions.explanation: Team leader identifies the post-operative patient as requiring the first visit due to wound monitoring.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: The post-op patient needs to be seen first — we need to check that wound.
questions.distractorExplanation: The diabetic and housebound patients are on the caseload but are not flagged as the priority.
optionDistractorWhy: On the visit list but lower priority today.
Routine diabetic visit — can be seen later in the day.

questions.stem: The main purpose of the new hand-hygiene policy is to
questions.explanation: Speaker states the policy's primary goal is to drive down healthcare-associated infection rates.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Our core objective is to reduce healthcare-associated infections across all wards.
questions.distractorExplanation: Cost savings and CQC compliance are mentioned as secondary benefits, not the stated purpose.
optionDistractorWhy: A secondary benefit acknowledged briefly, not the main driver.
Compliance is a consequence, not the stated reason for the policy.

questions.stem: The speaker's attitude toward staff compliance so far is best described as
questions.explanation: Speaker expresses frustration that audit data shows compliance rates remain below target.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I have to be honest — the audit figures are not where they should be and that disappoints me.
questions.distractorExplanation: She does not praise compliance nor remain neutral; her language signals clear disappointment.
optionDistractorWhy: Contradicted directly — she says figures are below target.
She uses evaluative language, not neutral reporting.

questions.stem: The PPE that is mandatory for all patient contact is
questions.explanation: Speaker specifies that both gloves and apron must be worn for every patient contact.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: For every patient contact — gloves and apron, without exception.
questions.distractorExplanation: Gloves alone are insufficient; full gown and mask are reserved for isolation procedures.
optionDistractorWhy: Incomplete — the policy explicitly adds apron to gloves.
Reserved for isolation rooms, not routine patient contact.

questions.stem: The training sessions are described as
questions.explanation: Speaker states attendance at training is compulsory for every member of clinical staff.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: These sessions are mandatory — every clinical member of staff, no exceptions.
questions.distractorExplanation: Voluntariness and restriction to new starters are both explicitly contradicted by the speaker.
optionDistractorWhy: Contradicted — 'mandatory' is used explicitly.
She says 'every clinical member of staff', not just new starters.

questions.stem: The consequence of non-compliance with the policy is
questions.explanation: Speaker states non-compliance will be referred to the formal disciplinary process.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Non-compliance will be referred for formal disciplinary proceedings.
questions.distractorExplanation: Verbal warnings and retraining are mentioned as initial responses but not the stated consequence.
optionDistractorWhy: Insufficient — the speaker escalates beyond a verbal warning.
Retraining is offered alongside discipline, not instead of it.

questions.stem: The speaker's main recommendation to all staff is to
options: consult their line manager before acting
questions.explanation: Speaker closes by urging staff to report every near-miss without delay to enable learning.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Report every near-miss the moment it happens — that's how we learn and improve.
questions.distractorExplanation: Line manager consultation and record updates are standard duties, not the closing recommendation.
optionDistractorWhy: She says act immediately, not wait for manager advice.
Routine admin task — not her closing call to action.

questions.stem: The speaker's main argument is that
options: psychiatrists should work in GP clinics
GPs are under-equipped to detect depression
antidepressants are over-prescribed in primary care
questions.correctAnswer: GPs are under-equipped to detect depression
questions.explanation: Speaker argues that GPs lack the tools and training to identify depression consistently.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: GPs are simply not equipped — they don't have the tools or the time to detect depression reliably.
questions.distractorExplanation: Co-locating psychiatrists is one proposed solution, not the main argument; prescribing is addressed separately.
optionDistractorWhy: A solution he proposes later, not his core argument.
He discusses prescribing as a consequence, not the central claim.

questions.stem: According to the speaker, a PHQ-9 score indicating moderate depression is
questions.explanation: Speaker cites PHQ-9 classification: scores 10–14 indicate moderate depression.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: A score of 10 to 14 on the PHQ-9 places someone in the moderate range.
questions.distractorExplanation: 5–9 is mild and 15–19 is moderately severe in the PHQ-9 classification.
optionDistractorWhy: This range corresponds to mild depression, not moderate.
This range indicates moderately severe, one step above moderate.

questions.stem: The speaker's attitude towards antidepressants is best described as
questions.explanation: Speaker acknowledges antidepressants are effective but stresses careful case selection.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: They work — when we use them for the right patients, at the right time.
questions.distractorExplanation: He does not oppose them nor advocate them without qualification.
optionDistractorWhy: He acknowledges clear benefit — opposition is contradicted.
He applies conditions; unqualified support is not his stance.

questions.stem: The recommended first-line treatment for mild depression is
questions.explanation: Speaker cites evidence that structured exercise is first-line for mild depression.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: For mild depression, the evidence points to structured exercise as the first thing we should offer.
questions.distractorExplanation: Antidepressants are second-line for mild cases; watchful waiting alone is insufficient.
optionDistractorWhy: He explicitly says medication should not be first-line for mild cases.
He argues doing nothing is not enough — intervention is needed.

questions.stem: According to the speaker, the biggest barrier to treatment is
questions.explanation: Speaker identifies patient stigma as the single largest obstacle to accessing mental health treatment.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Above all else, it is stigma — patients won't come forward because of what others might think.
questions.distractorExplanation: Cost and GP time are noted as systemic constraints but are ranked below stigma.
optionDistractorWhy: Mentioned as a system-level concern, not the biggest barrier.
Acknowledged but not ranked as the primary obstacle.

questions.stem: The speaker's conclusion is that we should
options: train specialist nurses in primary care
integrate mental health screening into annual health checks
questions.correctAnswer: integrate mental health screening into annual health checks
questions.explanation: Speaker closes by calling for mental health screening to be embedded in routine annual health reviews.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: We should embed mental health screening into the annual health check — that is my conclusion.
questions.distractorExplanation: Dedicated clinics and specialist nurses are proposals he considers but ultimately does not prioritise.
optionDistractorWhy: He views this as a longer-term structural change, not his immediate conclusion.
He mentions nurse training as supportive, not the core recommendation.
